macro_segments.title: Compromise (Pshara) and Binding Arbitration (Bitzua): Whether They Require a Kinyan and Whether They Are a Form of Din

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's inference that pshara does not require a kinyan based on the debate between those who say it requires three judges and those who say one

The apparent contradiction: if pshara requires a kinyan, why would three judges be necessary instead of two with a kinyan; and the resolution that pshara indeed requires a kinyan to bind the parties to a legitimate compromise

Tosafot's explanation that three judges are required because the compromise must be legitimate and responsible, not arbitrary, to justify binding both parties to a decision that may not be strictly according to law
The Baraita that pshara requires three judges just like din, and once judgment is finalized (gmar din), compromise is no longer permitted

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer ben Rabbi Yossi HaGlili's Position: Compromise Is Forbidden and Perverts Truth

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer ben Rabbi Yossi HaGlili forbids compromise (bitzua), calling it a perversion of truth, and interprets the verse about botze'a (one who breaks the loaf and blesses) as cursing God
Contrast between Moshe's absolute justice (yikov hadin et hahar) and Aharon's pursuit of peace (ohev shalom), illustrating two approaches to dispute resolution
Rashi's clarification that the prohibition on bitzua applies only after the parties come before the judges; beforehand, Aharon's pursuit of peace is praiseworthy
Digression: Rabbi Eliezer's teaching about stolen goods being used for a mitzvah (separating challah), which constitutes blasphemy rather than blessing
Rabbi Meir's application of the botze'a teaching to Yehuda's sale of Yosef, arguing that blessing such an act is blaspheming God

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha's Position: Compromise Is a Mitzvah When It Combines Truth, Judgment, and Peace

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha argues that compromise is a mitzvah, citing the verse 'truth and judgment and peace shall you judge in your gates'
The paradox that where there is judgment there is no peace and vice versa, and the resolution that compromise (bitzua) is the type of judgment that contains peace
The example of David doing both mishpat and tzedakah, understood as David rendering strict judgment while personally providing charity to those harmed by the judgment
Rabbi Yehoshua's alternative reading that even without the judge's personal payment, judgment itself can be tzedakah (righteousness) for the losing party by freeing them from ill-gotten goods

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Menasya's Position: Compromise Permitted Until the Direction of Judgment Becomes Clear

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Menasya permits compromise if the judge has not yet heard the claims or does not yet know which way the judgment will lean
Once the judge knows which direction the judgment is leaning (leheichan hadin noteh), compromise is no longer permitted, based on the verse 'releasing water is the beginning of contention'

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Position: A Judge May Recuse Themselves Before the Direction Is Clear

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish permits a judge to recuse himself (ani nizkak lachem) if he fears confrontation, but only before knowing which way the judgment is leaning
Once the judge knows the direction of judgment, recusal is forbidden and the judge must not fear people, as God's justice is paramount

macro_segments.title: Obligations of Students, Judges, and Witnesses to Speak Truth and Pursue Justice

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha teaches that a student sitting before a teacher must not be silent if they see merit for the poor or fault with the rich, based on the verse lo taguru (do not hold back)
Rabbi Chanina's teaching that one must not withhold their words out of fear of any person, even if not a judge
Witnesses must know who they testify for, before whom they testify (before God), and from whom God will exact payment
The verse 'let two stand before God' indicates that witnesses present themselves before God, and false testimony makes God work harder to restore justice
Similarly, judges must know whom they judge, before whom they judge, and who will exact from them; God stands in the assembly of judges
Josaphat's charge to judges that they judge not for man but for God, and the reassurance that God is with them and they are only responsible to judge according to what their eyes see

macro_segments.title: Definition of Gmar Din and the Halacha According to Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha

micro_segments.title: Gmar din (finalized judgment) occurs when the judges say 'so-and-so you are liable' or 'so-and-so you are exempt'
The halacha is ruled according to Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha that compromise is a mitzvah, evidenced by Rav Huna offering litigants both options (din or pshara)
Clarification that the mitzvah is to inform litigants of both options, not necessarily to encourage compromise; the Tanna Kamma permits compromise but does not actively promote it
The difference between the Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha is whether the judge must proactively present compromise as an option (mitzvah) or merely allow it if the parties request it

macro_segments.title: Dispute with Rabbi Tanchum bar Chanilai: The Botze'a Verse Applies to the Golden Calf, Not Compromise

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Tanchum bar Chanilai argues that the verse about botze'a (one who breaks the loaf and blesses) refers not to compromise but to Aharon's compromise with his own conscience regarding the Golden Calf
Aharon saw Chur slaughtered and feared the same fate; he compromised by making the calf, believing this would allow for repentance rather than the more heinous crime of murdering a kohen and prophet

The Gemara rejects the idea that אהרן Aharon's self-preservation motive alone justifies his compromise, arguing instead that by providing an outlet short of murder, he prevented an even greater desecration

macro_segments.title: Alternative Reading of 'Releasing Water Is the Beginning of Contention': Stages of Conflict and Their Remedies

micro_segments.title: The other Tanna'im who reject the interpretation of 'poter mayim reishit madon' as permission to recuse apply it instead to avoiding disputes over matters of Torah study
Rav Hamnuna's teaching that a person is first judged on whether he held back from Torah; 'releasing water' means exempting oneself from Torah study, which is the beginning of all judgment
The image of a fight like a cracked hose: once it widens, it cannot be easily repaired; therefore early intervention in disputes is crucial
Abaye's contrasting image of a bridge board: once established, it remains firm, suggesting that reconciliation becomes harder over time
A series of folk sayings and aphorisms about conflict, peace, and behavior, each supported by verses, illustrating how everyday wisdom aligns with Scripture
An aphorism that a person who ignores insults avoids a hundred misfortunes, supported by the verse that releasing water is the beginning of contention (100 in gematria)
An extended aphorism about love sustaining a couple even in poverty, contrasted with wealth failing to sustain a couple without love, supported by the verse about God speaking from the ark
Rav Huna's expansion showing that God's closeness to Israel (ten tefachim from the ground at the Ark) departed when Israel sinned, even the expanded Second Temple could not contain God

macro_segments.title: Etymology and Meanings of Lo Taguru: Gathering In Words and Testimony

micro_segments.title: The word taguru in lo taguru can mean to gather in or gather up, as seen in verses about wine and harvests, supporting the reading that one must not hold back words or testimony
Rav Acha bar Yaakov cites the verse about the industrious ant gathering food in harvest, showing that oger (to gather) confirms the meaning of gathering and accumulating
Rav Acha son of Rav Ika cites the verse about the wise son gathering in summer, further establishing that oger means to gather, reinforcing the sense that lo taguru means do not hold back

Reish Lakish / Rabbi Yehoshua ben Lakish
Botze'a (one who breaks bread and blesses; ill-gotten gains)
Emes umishpat shalom (truth and judgment and peace)
Lo taguru (do not hold back/do not be afraid)
Mishpat lelokim hu (the judgment belongs to God)
Leheichan hadin noteh (where the judgment is leaning)
Ani nizkak lachem (I am not dealing with you / recusal)
Poter mayim reishit madon (releasing water is the beginning of contention)
